1: רַנְּנוּ צַדִּיקִים בַּיהוָה (rannĕnû ṣaddîqîm baYHWH, "Shout for joy in the LORD, you righteous!") — Psalm 33 has no superscription, making it anonymous — the only anonymous psalm in Book I (Ps 1-41) alongside Ps 1, 2, and 10. In the LXX it is attributed to David. The opening rinnāh (ringing shout of joy) picks up directly from Psalms 32:11, continuing the exuberant sound. לַיְשָׁרִים נָאוָה תְהִלָּה (layyĕshārîm nāwāh tĕhillāh, "Praise is beautiful from those who are upright") — nāwāh: fitting, beautiful, appropriate. Praise is the congruous response of the yĕshārîm (upright ones).
2: הוֹדוּ לַיהוָה בְּכִנּוֹר (hôdû laYHWH bĕkinnôr, "Praise the LORD with the lyre") — kinnôr: the lyre — David's instrument (1 Samuel 16:23), the principal string instrument of ancient Israel. בְּנֵבֶל עָשׂוֹר זַמְּרוּ-לוֹ (bĕnêbel ʿāśôr zammĕrû-lô, "make music with the ten-stringed harp") — nêbel: the harp (larger than the kinnôr); ʿāśôr: "ten" (ten-stringed). The physicality of worship: instruments, voices, hands — all engaged.
3: שִׁירוּ-לוֹ שִׁיר חָדָשׁ (shîrû-lô shîr ḥādāsh, "Sing him a new song") — ḥādāsh: new — the new song that corresponds to new saving acts (Psalms 96:1; Psalms 98:1; Psalms 149:1; Isaiah 42:10; Revelation 5:9; Psalms 14:3). Not novelty for its own sake but the appropriate response to fresh divine action. הֵיטִיבוּ נַגֵּן בִּתְרוּעָה (hêṭîbû naggên bitĕrûʿāh, "play with skill and shout for joy") — yāṭab (hiphil: to do well, to be skilled): excellence in musical execution as part of worship. Tĕrûʿāh: the war-shout/trumpet blast of victory — the same word as the shout at Jericho.
4: כִּי-יָשָׁר דְּבַר-יְהוָה (kî-yāshār dĕbar-YHWH, "For the word of the LORD is right and true") — yāshār: straight, right, upright — the divine word as the standard of uprightness. וְכָל-מַעֲשֵׂהוּ בֶּאֱמוּנָה (wĕkol-maʿăśêhû beʾĕmûnāh, "all his works are done in faithfulness") — ʾĕmûnāh: faithfulness, reliability, consistency. The word is straight; the works are faithful. This verse prepares for vv6-9 by establishing that the creative word operates in faithfulness.
5: אֹהֵב צְדָקָה וּמִשְׁפָּט (ʾōhêb ṣĕdāqāh ûmishpāṭ, "He loves righteousness and justice") — the pair ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness/right-standing) and mishpāṭ (justice/judgment) is the foundational moral vocabulary of the Hebrew prophets (Amos 5:24; Micah 6:8; Isaiah 5:7). God's love (ʾāhab) for these qualities expresses his character. חֶסֶד יְהוָה מָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ (ḥesed YHWH māleʾāh hāʾāreṣ, "the earth is full of the LORD's steadfast love") — the ḥesed of YHWH fills the earth — not private and limited to Israel but overflowing into the whole created order.
6: בִּדְבַר יְהוָה שָׁמַיִם נַעֲשׂוּ (bidĕbar YHWH shāmayim naʿăśû, "The heavens were made by the word of the LORD") — dĕbar YHWH: the word of the LORD — the creative speech of Gen 1 ("and God said…"). The heavens are the product of divine speech. וּבְרוּחַ פִּיו כָּל-צְבָאָם (ûbĕrûaḥ pîw kol-ṣĕbāʾām, "all their host by the breath of his mouth") — rûaḥ pîw: "breath/spirit of his mouth" — the divine breath that is both the wind hovering over the waters (Genesis 1:2) and the speech-breath issuing creative words. John 1:1-3 develops this dĕbar YHWH theology christologically: the Word (Logos) through whom all things were made is the pre-existent Son.
7: כֹּנֵס כַּנֵּד מֵי הַיָּם (kōnês kannêd mê hayyām, "He gathers the sea's waters like a heap") — kānās: to gather, to pile up; nêd: a heap, a mound. This echoes the Exodus sea-crossing: "The waters piled up; the surging waters stood firm like a wall" (Exodus 15:8, the same word nêd). God's creative ordering of the waters is the same power as the Exodus rescue. נֹתֵן בְּאֹצָרוֹת תְּהוֹמוֹת (nōtên bĕʾōṣārôt tĕhômôt, "he stores the deep in reservoirs") — tĕhôm: the deep (Genesis 1:2); ʾōṣār: treasury, storehouse. God stores the chaotic deep as if it were treasure in a vault — sovereignty over the primordial chaos.
8: יִירְאוּ מֵיְהוָה כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (yîrĕʾû mêYHWH kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Let all the earth fear the LORD") — the universal summons to yirʾāh (reverence/fear): not Israel alone but all the earth. The ground for universal reverence is given in vv6-7: the creator of the heavens and sovereign over the deep deserves universal acknowledgment. מִמֶּנּוּ יָגוּרוּ כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי תֵבֵל (mimmennu yāgûrû kol-yōshĕbê têbêl, "let all who live in the world stand in awe of him") — têbêl: the inhabited world — the specifically human-populated earth.
9: כִּי הוּא אָמַר וַיֶּהִי (kî hûʾ ʾāmar wayyehî, "For he spoke, and it came to be") — the fiat-creation formula: ʾāmar (he spoke) and yehî (and it was) — the Gen 1 pattern: "And God said… and there was." The word is causally sufficient for existence: no further mechanism needed. הוּא-צִוָּה וַיַּעֲמֹד (hûʾ-ṣiwwāh wayyaʿămōd, "he commanded, and it stood firm") — ṣiwwāh: to command (the same word for divine commandments); ʿāmad: to stand, to endure. What the divine command calls into being "stands" — the command is the foundation of created permanence.
10: יְהוָה הֵפִיר עֲצַת-גּוֹיִם (YHWH hêpîr ʿăṣat-gôyim, "The LORD thwarts the purposes of the nations") — pārar (hiphil: to break, to frustrate, to make void): the divine frustration of human plans. ʿĒṣāh: counsel, plan, strategy. הֵנִיא מַחְשְׁבוֹת עַמִּים (hênîʾ maḥshĕbôt ʿammîm, "he brings the plans of the peoples to nothing") — maḥshāb: thought, plan (from ḥāshab: to reckon — the same accounting root as Psalms 32:2). The nations' reckonings are un-reckoned by the LORD; their plans dissolve before the divine purpose (v11).
11: עֲצַת יְהוָה לְעוֹלָם תַּעֲמֹד (ʿăṣat YHWH lĕʿôlām taʿămōd, "The counsel of the LORD stands forever") — the same word ʿēṣāh (counsel/purpose) used for the nations' plans in v10, now applied to the divine plan. The contrast is permanence: the nations' ʿēṣāh is frustrated; the LORD's ʿēṣāh stands (ʿāmad) forever — the same verb as v9 ("it stood firm"). מַחְשְׁבוֹת לִבּוֹ לְדֹר וָדֹר (maḥshĕbôt libbô lĕdōr wādōr, "the purposes of his heart through all generations") — the deep interior intention of God persists generation after generation.
12: אַשְׁרֵי הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר-יְהוָה אֱלֹהָיו (ʾashrê haggôy ʾăsher-YHWH ʾĕlōhāyw, "Blessed is the nation whose God is the LORD") — the ʾashrê beatitude applied to a nation (gôy): collective blessedness. הָעָם בָּחַר לְנַחֲלָה לוֹ (hāʿām bāḥar lĕnaḥălāh lô, "the people he has chosen as his own possession") — bāḥar: to choose (the election vocabulary of Deuteronomy 7:6); naḥălāh: inheritance, possession. The chosen people as the LORD's own inheritance. 1 Peter 2:9 applies this election-language to the church: "a chosen people… God's special possession."
13: מִשָּׁמַיִם הִבִּיט יְהוָה (mishshāmayim hibbîṭ YHWH, "The LORD looks down from heaven") — nābaṭ (hiphil: to look, to gaze intently): not a casual glance but deliberate, attentive observation from the divine vantage above the heavens. רָאָה אֶת-כָּל-בְּנֵי הָאָדָם (rāʾāh ʾet-kol-bĕnê hāʾādām, "he observes all humanity") — bĕnê hāʾādām: "sons of Adam" — humanity as the descendants of the first human. Universal scope: not Israel specifically but all of Adam's offspring.
14: מִמְּכוֹן-שִׁבְתּוֹ הִשְׁגִּיחַ (mimmĕkôn-shībtô hishgîaḥ, "from his dwelling place he surveys") — shāgaḥ (hiphil: to look attentively, to survey): the patient, comprehensive gaze. Mĕkôn-shîbtô: "the established/fixed place of his sitting" — the heavenly throne as the permanent vantage point. אֶל כָּל-יֹשְׁבֵי הָאָרֶץ (ʾel kol-yōshĕbê hāʾāreṣ, "all who live on the earth") — every earthly inhabitant within the divine field of vision.
15: הַיֹּצֵר יַחַד לִבָּם (hayyōṣêr yaḥad libbām, "he who forms the hearts of all") — yāṣar: to form, to shape (as a potter shapes clay — Genesis 2:7: "the LORD God formed the human from the dust"). Yaḥad libbām: "all their hearts together" — the divine potter who formed each individual heart also knows what he has made. הַמֵּבִין אֶל-כָּל-מַעֲשֵׂיהֶם (hammêbîn ʾel-kol-maʿăśêhem, "and considers everything they do") — bîn: to understand, to discern (Psalms 28:5; Psalms 32:9). The one who made the hearts understands every deed those hearts produce.
16: אֵין-הַמֶּלֶךְ נוֹשָׁע בְּרָב-חָיִל (ʾên-hammelek nôshāʿ bĕrab-ḥāyil, "No king is saved by the size of his army") — political theology: military might (ḥāyil: valor, army) does not determine victory. Nôshāʿ (niphal passive: saved/rescued): the king himself is not the saver but the one who needs saving — and the size of his forces does not guarantee his rescue.
17: שֶׁקֶר הַסּוּס לִתְשׁוּעָה (sheqer hassûs litshûʿāh, "A horse is a vain hope for victory") — sheqer: falsehood, deception — the horse as a liar in the sense of promising what it cannot deliver. The war-horse (sûs) was the cutting-edge military technology of the ancient world (chariot warfare); Psalms 20:7 contrasted horse-trust with name-of-the-LORD trust. וּבְרֹב חֵילוֹ לֹא יְמַלֵּט (ûbĕrōb ḥêlô lōʾ yĕmallêṭ, "its great power cannot bring rescue") — mālat (piel: to cause to escape, to rescue): the horse's great strength cannot produce escape.
18: הִנֵּה עֵין יְהוָה אֶל-יְרֵאָיו (hinnêh ʿên YHWH ʾel-yĕrêʾāyw, "The eye of the LORD is on those who fear him") — the divine eye that surveys all humanity (v13-14) is specifically protective toward those who fear him. Yĕrêʾāyw: those who fear him — the relationship vocabulary. לַמְיַחֲלִים לְחַסְדּוֹ (lamĕyaḥălîm lĕḥasdô, "on those who hope in his steadfast love") — yāḥal: to wait with expectation (the same word as the closing line of Psalms 31:24); ḥesed: covenant love. The eye is on those whose hope is directed toward the LORD's own ḥesed — not toward their own resources (vv16-17).
19: לְהַצִּיל מִמָּוֶת נַפְשָׁם (lĕhaṣṣîl mimmāwet napshām, "to rescue them from death") — the purpose of the divine eye: rescue from death. Nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away, rescue). Nāpesh: the living self — the whole person rescued from death. וּלְחַיּוֹתָם בָּרָעָב (ûlĕḥayyôtām bārāʿāb, "to keep them alive in famine") — concrete provision: survival in famine (rāʿāb). God's providential care is not abstract — it reaches into the physical need for food.
20: נַפְשֵׁנוּ חִכְּתָה לַיהוָה (napshênû ḥikkĕtāh laYHWH, "We wait for the LORD") — the communal voice: "our soul has waited" (ḥikkāh: to wait, to be patient — intensive waiting). The individual hope of v18 now becomes the communal confession. עֶזְרֵנוּ וּמָגִנֵּנוּ הוּא (ʿezrênû ûmāginnênû hûʾ, "he is our help and our shield") — ʿezer: helper (Psalms 30:10); māgên: shield (Psalms 28:7). The communal declaration: "he is" — the predicate of identity, not petition. Already the help; already the shield.
21: כִּי-בוֹ יִשְׂמַח לִבֵּנוּ (kî-bô yiśmaḥ libbênû, "Our hearts are glad in him") — śāmaḥ: rejoice, be glad; the gladness is "in him" (bô) — not in circumstances but in the person of the LORD who is the help and shield. כִּי בְשֵׁם קָדְשׁוֹ בָטָחְנוּ (kî bĕshêm qodshô bāṭāḥnû, "for we trust in his holy name") — bāṭaḥ: to trust, to lean on (the persistent trust-verb of this cluster of psalms). The holy name as the ground of trust.
22: יְהִי-חַסְדְּךָ יְהוָה עָלֵינוּ (yĕhî-ḥasdĕkā YHWH ʿālênû, "Let your steadfast love, LORD, rest on us") — the jussive closing prayer: may the ḥesed that belongs to the LORD come to rest on the community. The psalm ends with the same word that saturated it — ḥesed (vv5, 18, 22). The whole psalm is a meditation on divine ḥesed as the ground of creation (v5), the object of hope (v18), and the content of the closing prayer. כַּאֲשֶׁר יִחַלְנוּ לָךְ (kaʾăsher yiḥalnû lāk, "as we have placed our hope in you") — yāḥal: the same hope-waiting of v18 and Psalms 31:24. The closing prayer links the ḥesed to the hoping: may covenant love rest on us in proportion to the hope we have placed in you.
